claims.claim: SEI membrane decomposition is considered the first step in thermal runaway and is an exothermic process whose released heat accumulates inside the battery, accelerating thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The decomposition of SEI membranes is widely considered to be the first step in thermal runaway. SEI membrane decomposition is an exothermic process, and the heat released from decomposition will accumulate inside the battery, accelerating the occurrence of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Microcalorimetry experiments showed two exothermic peaks during SEI decomposition occurring at approximately 45 degrees Celsius and 100 degrees Celsius.
claims.quote: The results of the microcalorimetry experiment show that there are two exothermic peaks. These peaks occur at temperatures approximately around 45 degrees centigrade and 100 degrees centigrade.

claims.claim: At 105 degrees Celsius, the SEI film appears dispersed and incomplete, indicating that high temperatures cause the SEI film to undergo decomposition leading to a looser structure.
claims.quote: after treatment at temperature of 105 degrees centigrade, the SEI film appears dispersed and incomplete. This indicates that at high temperatures, the SEI film undergoes decomposition to some extent, leading to a looser structure.

claims.claim: The SEI impedance value dropped by more than 70% when the temperature reached 75 degrees Celsius compared to the room temperature reference value, making SEI impedance more sensitive than battery terminal voltage to temperature changes.
claims.quote: dropped more than 70%. Through the analysis, it can be seen that the SEI impedance is more sensitive than the battery voltage when temperature changes. Thereafter, comparing with the battery terminal voltage, it is better to use the SEI impedance as a proper sign for the thermal runaway risk of the battery.

claims.claim: The impedance value of the SEI component (P2) almost disappeared when the holding time exceeded 20 minutes at 105 degrees Celsius, indicating severe damage or complete disappearance of the SEI film.
claims.quote: The impedance value of P2 almost disappeared when the holding time exceeded 20 minutes when the cell is hold at 105 degrees Celsius. At this time, the structure of SEI film may be seriously damaged or completely disappeared.

claims.claim: The exothermic decomposition of the SEI around 100–150°C has been reported to initiate thermal runaway in lithium ion batteries.
claims.quote: The exothermic decomposition of the SEI around 100~150oC has been reported to initiate thermal runaway in LIBs.

claims.claim: LEDC thermally decomposes over the temperature range of 50–300°C to evolve CO2 and C2H4 gases, leaving lithium propionate (CH3CH2CO2Li) and Li2CO3 as solid residues.
claims.quote: Over the temperature range of 50~300oC, LEDC decomposes to evolve CO2 and C2H4 gasses leaving lithium propionate (CH3CH2CO2Li) and Li2CO3 as solid residues.

claims.claim: Lithium propionate decomposes over the temperature range of 300–600°C to evolve pentanone, leaving Li2CO3 as a residual solid.
claims.quote: The lithium propionate decomposes over the temperature range of 300~600oC to evolve pentanone leaving Li2CO3 as a residual solid.

claims.claim: Li2CO3 decomposes above 600°C to evolve CO2, leaving Li2O as a residual solid.
claims.quote: Finally, the Li2CO3 decomposes over 600oC to evolve CO2 leaving Li2O as a residual solid.

claims.claim: TGA measurements of Si electrodes after various numbers of cycles suggest that LEDC on Si electrodes thermally decomposes during cycling to form lithium propionate and Li2CO3, resulting in increased complexity of the SEI.
claims.quote: TGA measurements of Si electrodes after various numbers of cycles suggest that the LEDC on Si electrodes thermally decomposes during cycling to form lithium propionate and Li2CO3, resulting in increased complexity of the SEI.

claims.claim: The Li2CO3 decomposition in the SEI on cycled Si electrodes begins at a lower temperature than that for independently prepared LEDC, which is attributed to the high content of carbon in the Si electrode causing carbothermal reduction.
claims.quote: The Li2CO3 decomposition in the SEI begins at a lower temperature than that for the independently prepared LEDC (Figure 8a). This can be attributed to high content of carbon in the Si electrode. Carbon has been known to lead to carbothermal reduction, altering the decomposition kinetics and products, while thermal decomposition of pure Li2CO3 is known to begin above 800oC.

claims.claim: The weight loss in the 250–350°C range in cycled Si electrodes results from the thermal decomposition of LixPOyFz, a decomposition product of LiPF6 reported as a component of the SEI.
claims.quote: the thermal decomposition of LixPOyFz, a decomposition product LiPF6 which has frequently been reported as a component of the SEI, has been investigated. Independently obtained or prepared lithium phosphate derivatives undergo thermal decomposition around 300oC... the weight loss in the 250~350oC range results from the thermal decomposition of LixPOyFz.

claims.claim: The peaks characteristic of RCO2Li and Li2CO3 show greater increases in intensity between the 30th and 50th cycles than that observed for LEDC, suggesting that RCO2Li and Li2CO3 were present in the SEI prior to TGA measurement, consistent with LEDC thermally decomposing during prolonged cycling.
claims.quote: the intensity ratios between LEDC, RCO2Li, and Li2CO3 change as a function of cycling. In particular, the peaks characteristic of RCO2Li and Li2CO3 have greater increases in intensity between the 30th and 50th cycles than that observed for LEDC. This suggests that not all the RCO2Li and Li2CO3 are products of LEDC decomposition during the TGA experiment. Rather, the RCO2Li and Li2CO3 were present in the SEI prior to the TGA measurement. This is consistent with LEDC thermally decomposing during prolonged cycling to generate RCO2Li, and Li2CO3 in the SEI.

claims.claim: Ethylene (C2H4) evolution from LEDC clearly begins at approximately 90°C before CO2 evolution, suggesting that an intermediate, lithium peroxodicarbonate (Li2C2O6), is generated.
claims.quote: it is interesting that ethylene evolution clearly begins at ~90oC (2 min at 20oC min-1) before CO2 evolution suggesting that an intermediate, lithium peroxodicarbonate (Li2C2O6), is generated.

claims.claim: The thermal decomposition of LEDC could be accelerated by the surface of the lithiated silicon electrode or acidic decomposition products of the LiPF6 electrolyte.
claims.quote: The LEDC decomposition could be accelerated by the surface of the lithiated silicon electrode or acidic decomposition products of the LiPF6 electrolyte.

claims.claim: The ratio of triggering energy of self-heat onset to total self-heat generation remained consistent across LFP batteries with a mean value of 24.5%, indicating that LFP batteries obtain approximately 80% of their energy from internal exothermic reactions during adiabatic thermal abuse.
claims.quote: The mean value of the ratio was 24.5%, indicating that lithium iron phosphate batteries obtain most of the energy (generally 80%) from internal exothermic reactions during adiabatic thermal abuse.

claims.claim: The triggering energy of thermal runaway remained constant at approximately 670 kJ when various heating powers (300–600 W) were applied to a 280 Ah LFP battery, with an inaccuracy of only 2.5%, accounting for 20.8% of the theoretical energy stored inside.
claims.quote: TE of thermal runaway remains constant when multiple heating powers are used to activate thermal runaway and the inaccuracy of triggering energy is only 2.5%... For the 280 Ah LFP battery investigated in this paper, 670 kJ is the minimum triggering energy of thermal runaway, accounting for 20.8% theoretical energy stored inside.

claims.claim: Approximately 65.5% of the self-heat generation in LFP batteries is released during thermal runaway in a short period of time, causing a steep rise in temperature.
claims.quote: This means almost 65.5% of the self-heat will be released in a short time, leading to a steep rise in the temperature.

claims.claim: Increasing the number of cells (thinner cells) inside a battery accelerates thermal runaway propagation by increasing the average temperature of each thinner cell, with cell temperature and heat generation having positive feedback.
claims.quote: the thinner the cells, the faster thermal runaway propagates... more cells and thinner cells can accelerate the thermal runaway propagation by increasing the average temperature of the thinner cells. This serves as a reminder to manufacturers that to offer safer performance in multi-cell setups, an additional thermal-isolation layer must be placed between two cells.

claims.claim: Battery thickness has little impact on maximum temperature but causes thermal runaway triggering time and time to maximum temperature to grow linearly with battery thickness.
claims.quote: the battery thickness has little impact on the maximum temperature but changes the thermal runaway process in terms of triggering time for thermal runaway Tonset. The triggering time for thermal runaway and the time required to achieve the maximum temperature grow linearly with battery thickness.
